summary: This notebook demonstrates a multi-model fine-tuning and inference pipeline for the Jigsaw Agile Community Rules competition. It fine-tunes a Qwen2.5-0.5B model with LoRA for direct classification, runs constrained decoding inference on a Qwen2.5-14B model, and explores a semantic retrieval approach using a fine-tuned Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B model. The author combines these three distinct approaches via rank-based blending to produce a final submission. Key takeaways include effective few-shot prompt engineering, the reliability of constrained decoding via logits processors, and the performance gains from blending generation and embedding-based models of different scales.
competition_flow: Loads Reddit comment data with rules and examples, fine-tunes Qwen2.5-0.5B via LoRA and Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B, runs constrained decoding inference on both plus a Qwen2.5-14B baseline, and blends the ranked predictions into a final submission.
data_reading: Randomly samples 50% of test data for training augmentation
Constructs prompts by concatenating subreddit, rule, randomly selected positive/negative examples, and the target comment
data_processing: Randomly selects one positive and one negative example per row for training/test
Applies cleantext.clean for text normalization in the embedding pipeline
Uses completion_only_loss=True during training to mask prompt tokens
Applies softmax to logprobs in one inference script
Normalizes predictions via rank-based scaling rank/(N+1)
ensembling: Combines predictions from a LoRA-finetuned Qwen2.5-0.5B, a semantic retrieval model (Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B), and a Qwen2.5-14B baseline by ranking each submission, normalizing scores, and blending them with weights 0.5, 0.3, and 0.2 respectively.
insights: Few-shot prompt formatting with randomly selected positive/negative examples improves classification consistency across different rules.
Constrained decoding via MultipleChoiceLogitsProcessor ensures valid Yes/No outputs without sampling overhead or hallucination.
Rank-based normalization and blending across models of different sizes and paradigms (generation vs. embedding) yields more robust predictions than any single model.
